title: Whittled sycamore cocktail picks
subtitle: A set of six pointed picks from a single green sycamore billet
excerpt: Six cocktail picks from one small green sycamore billet: each pick 12 cm long, with a tapered point at one end and a flat paddle handle at the other. The project introduces straight-taper whittling; the technique behind whistle sticks, tent pegs, and skewers. The full set takes an afternoon.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for skewer and pick forms; whittled picks are documented in Victorian domestic craft manuals.

content.text: Cocktail picks are the simplest whittling project that produces a usable object. The skill is the straight-taper: a long, even reduction from the full diameter of the billet at the handle end to a sharp point at the tip. That taper is what a skewer, a wooden tent peg, a bobbin, and a spindle all share; learning to cut it cleanly is the foundation of a dozen other projects.
Green
sycamore is the correct choice; it splits into thin clean billets and the wet wood carves without resistance. Six picks from one billet means six chances to refine the taper.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and slicing cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest.
Sharpening: strop every 15 minutes. A dull knife on a thin pick will skip rather than shave.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

A small green sycamore billet: 15 cm long, 5 cm square. Split it by hand along the grain into six rectangular strips, each roughly 12 mm wide and 8 mm deep, with the grain running the length. The strips will not be perfectly even; that is fine. Each strip becomes one pick.
Method: one pick
Work the strip to a round or oval cross-section using
push cuts
along the length. Hold the strip in a four-finger grip with the blade pointing away from the body. Each cut removes one of the corners of the rectangular cross-section in turn.

Mark the handle end: the flat-paddle section, 4 cm long from the blunt end, tapered to a paddle shape on both faces. Carve the paddle with push cuts across the face of the strip. The paddle gives the pick something to grip and distinguishes the handle end from the point end.
Taper the point end. Begin the taper 6 cm from the tip. Use
slicing cuts
rather than straight push cuts on the final 2 cm to the tip: the knife edge drawn at 20 degrees to the long axis of the pick, slicing rather than pushing. This produces a cleaner taper on end-grain.

The point should be sharp enough to push into a cocktail olive without splitting it. If the point splits rather than pierces, the taper is too abrupt; re-cut the final centimetre with a shallower slicing angle.
Leave the six picks to dry flat for 1 week before finishing. Green sycamore picks dry quickly and without cracking; the small mass and thin section lose moisture quickly.
Finishing

Sand the paddle and shaft with 220 grit. Apply a thin coat of raw linseed oil; wipe off after 30 minutes. Cure 2 weeks before food contact. The point can remain unsanded; knife-cut facets on a cocktail pick are part of the character.
Variations
Hazel: dries slightly faster, same method.
Decorative knob handle: replace the paddle with a carved ball or acorn-cap finial at the blunt end. The same slicing-taper technique produces the ball.
Care

Wash with warm water after each use. Towel-dry at once. Re-oil every few months. Replace any pick that shows surface mould or a crack at the point; the narrow section concentrates any structural weakness.

items.fix: Rotate the picks daily during drying so both faces get equal air contact. A slight bow in a 12 mm strip is usually too minor to affect use.
items.cause: Grain running slightly off-axis in the strip; or one face dried faster than the other
items.symptom: Pick bends rather than holding straight during drying

items.fix: Extend the taper further back up the shaft. The point needs to be gradual; a tip that looks sharp but has a sudden shoulder 2 cm back will crack at the shoulder.
items.cause: Taper too abrupt; the taper ended at 2 cm from the tip rather than 6 cm
items.symptom: Point splits when pressed into food
